---
title: "class11"
author: "Joshua Chun (A17812847)"
format: pdf
toc: true
---

```{r}
# install.packages("bio3d")
# install.packages("pheatmap")
# install.packages("jsonlite")

library(bio3d)
library(pheatmap)
library(jsonlite)
```

```{r}
results_dir <- "hivpr_dimer/"
```

```{r}
results_dir <- "hivpr_dimer"
pdb_files <- list.files(path=results_dir, pattern="\\.pdb$", full.names=TRUE)
basename(pdb_files)
```


```{r}
# Align and superpose all AlphaFold models
pdbs <- pdbaln(pdb_files,
               fit = TRUE,
               exefile = "msa")

# Print alignment summary
pdbs
```

```{r}
# Calculating pairwise RMSD between all models
rd <- rmsd(pdbs, fit = TRUE)

# Printing RMSD range
range(rd)
```

```{r}
# Rows and columns for  labels
colnames(rd) <- paste0("m", seq_len(ncol(rd)))
rownames(rd) <- paste0("m", seq_len(nrow(rd)))

# Drawing RMSD heatmap
pheatmap(rd)
```
The RMSD heatmap compares structural similarity between the AlphaFold models. Lower RMSD values show more similar structures, while higher values indicate greater structural differences between models.

```{r}
# Reading experimental HIV protease structure
pdb <- read.pdb("1hsg")
```

```{r}
# Plotting pLDDT confidence scores for all models
plotb3(pdbs$b[1,],
       typ = "l",
       lwd = 2,
       sse = pdb)

points(pdbs$b[2,], typ = "l", col = "red")
points(pdbs$b[3,], typ = "l", col = "blue")
points(pdbs$b[4,], typ = "l", col = "darkgreen")
points(pdbs$b[5,], typ = "l", col = "orange")

# Add divider between the two chains
abline(v = 100, col = "gray")
```
The pLDDT scores represent AlphaFold confidence values for each residue position. Most regions show high confidence values, showing reliable structure predictions. Lower confidence regions  correspond to flexible or disordered regions.

```{r}
# Find rigid core positions shared across all models
core <- core.find(pdbs)

# Print core residue positions
core.inds <- print(core, vol = 0.5)
```
```{r}
# Superpose structures using only rigid core residues
xyz <- pdbfit(pdbs,
              core.inds,
              outpath = "corefit_structures")
```

```{r}
# Calculate RMSF values
rf <- rmsf(xyz)

# Plot RMSF across residue positions
plotb3(rf,
       sse = pdb,
       ylab = "RMSF")

# Add divider between chains
abline(v = 100, col = "gray")
```
The RMSF plot shows structural variability across residue positions. Higher RMSF values indicate regions with greater conformational variation between models.

```{r}
# Find all JSON files containing PAE information
pae_files <- list.files(path = results_dir,
                        pattern = ".*model.*\\.json",
                        full.names = TRUE)

# Print JSON file names
basename(pae_files)
```

```{r}
# Read PAE information for model 1 and model 5
pae1 <- read_json(pae_files[1],
                  simplifyVector = TRUE)

pae5 <- read_json(pae_files[5],
                  simplifyVector = TRUE)

# Show stored information
attributes(pae1)
```

```{r}
# Print maximum PAE scores
pae1$max_pae
pae5$max_pae
```
Low maximum PAE values indicate better confidence in the relative positioning of residues and domains. Model 1 has a lower maximum PAE value than model 5, indicating somewhat that model 1 is more reliable overall.

```{r}
# Plot PAE matrix for model 1
plot.dmat(pae1$pae,
          xlab = "Residue Position (i)",
          ylab = "Residue Position (j)",
          grid.col = "black",
          zlim = c(0, 30))
```

```{r}
# Plot PAE matrix for model 5
plot.dmat(pae5$pae,
          xlab = "Residue Position (i)",
          ylab = "Residue Position (j)",
          grid.col = "black",
          zlim = c(0, 30))
```

The PAE plots visualize AlphaFold confidence in the relative positioning between residues. Lower values indicate higher confidence. Model 1 shows generally lower PAE values, showing better predicted structural reliability than model 5.

```{r}
# Find the multiple sequence alignment file
aln_file <- list.files(path = results_dir,
                       pattern = ".a3m$",
                       full.names = TRUE)

# Print alignment file name
aln_file
```

```{r}
# Read the alignment file
aln <- read.fasta(aln_file[1],
                  to.upper = TRUE)

# Check alignment dimensions
dim(aln$ali)
```

```{r}
# Calculate residue conservation scores
sim <- conserv(aln)
```

```{r}
# Plot conservation scores for the first HIV protease chain
plotb3(sim[1:99],
       sse = trim.pdb(pdb, chain = "A"),
       ylab = "Conservation Score")
```

Highly conserved residues likely play important functional or structural roles in HIV protease. Conserved regions may correspond to catalytic residues or residues important for maintaining protein stability.

```{r}
# Generate consensus sequence using a high conservation cutoff
con <- consensus(aln,
                 cutoff = 0.9)

# Print consensus sequence
con$seq
```

The consensus sequence highlights highly conserved residues across the alignment. Strongly conserved positions are pretty functionally important.

```{r}
# Read the top-ranked AlphaFold model
m1.pdb <- read.pdb(pdb_files[1])

# Get the number of unique residues in the PDB model
n.res <- length(unique(m1.pdb$atom$resno))

# Use only the conservation scores that match the number of residues
sim.use <- sim[1:n.res]

# Map conservation scores onto the residue numbers in the PDB
occ <- vec2resno(sim.use, m1.pdb$atom$resno)

# Write a new PDB file with conservation scores in the occupancy column
write.pdb(m1.pdb,
          o = occ,
          file = "m1_conserv.pdb")
```

![HIV protease conservation structure](M1_CONSERV.PDB.png)
HIV protease AlphaFold model colored by residue conservation scores. Highly conserved active site residues are visible in the central region of the structure.

## Summary

AlphaFold successfully predicted HIV protease structures with generally high confidence scores. RMSD and RMSF analyses showed structural similarities and variable regions among models. PAE analysis demonstrated that some models were more reliable than others. Conservation analysis identified highly conserved residues that are likely important for HIV protease function and catalytic activity.
